title: Spacing: Give the Ball Room to Breathe

lesson.title: Why good offense is mostly geometry
lesson.body_md:
## Spacing is the secret

Watch the best offenses in the world. The thing they have in common isn't fancy plays — it's that the **floor is balanced**. Every player is in a spot where they can punish the defense if the ball comes their way.

Bad spacing = clogged paint = no drives = no kick-outs = no offense.

## The 5 spots

A balanced offensive set has someone in each of five spots:

1. **Top of the key** (the slot)
2. **Both wings** (45 degrees out)
3. **Both corners** (the strong-side and weak-side corner)

This is the **5-out** alignment, and it's the cleanest way to learn spacing. When the floor looks like a five-pointed star, every drive has somewhere to kick.

## The spacing rule

> *"Stay 12–15 feet from the next nearest teammate."*

Closer, and your defenders are close enough to help on each other. Farther, and the pass is too long and gets picked off.

## What kills spacing

1. **Bunching up.** Two players in the same area = both defenders sag into the paint.
2. **Drifting in.** Off-ball players who slide toward the ball give the help defender a freebie.
3. **Standing on the baseline.** The corner is the best spot in basketball. Step on the line, the play dies.
4. **Two cutters at once.** Pick a lane.

## Spacing during a drive

When your teammate drives, the floor changes:

- **Strong-side corner:** Drop deeper. Don't lift up.
- **Strong-side wing (above the drive):** Lift to the slot to give an outlet.
- **Top of the key:** Slide to the opposite slot to reverse.
- **Weak-side corner:** Stay. The skip pass is coming if the help comes off you.
- **Weak-side wing:** Lift slightly to give a kick-out option.

This is **drift and lift**. Off-ball players move just enough to keep their defender from helping.

## The 3 questions before every cut

1. **Am I dragging my defender into the paint with me?**  
   If yes, you're killing the drive. Stay where you are.
2. **Will my new spot give the driver an outlet?**  
   If yes, move there. If not, hold.
3. **Is anyone else already in this spot?**  
   If yes, find a different one.

## Why this matters more than skill

A team of great players with bad spacing loses to a team of average players with great spacing. Drives die. Passes get tipped. The defense gets to load up on every action.

## What you're training

Spacing scenarios put you off-ball during a developing play. You have to choose where to stand. The wrong spot makes the offense break. The right spot makes your driver look like a star.

## Coach's cue

> *"Pass and move. Drive and space. Never stand still where you started."*

Good spacing is invisible work — but it's the reason every great offense looks easy.
